title: Test design for rising tone cancellation

content.text:
Based on the spec @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and existing tests on @src/lex.zig discuss with me to write the content of test "expect State.add cancels an existing rising tone for representative cases and switch to literal input".

- Treat spec as source of truth.
- Take existing tests for code pattern, technique, convention. Note: use Span to seed the buffer_effective, no need to define new struct.
- Never touch production code.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.

content.thinking: I'll start by reviewing the spec and existing tests to get oriented.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
10:   input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
11:   don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
12: - When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
13:   character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
14:   Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
15:   lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
16: - Literal input remains until the new word.
17: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
18: 
19: ### Diacritics
20: 
21: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
22: Tones have a separate section.
23: 
24: #### Empty
25: 
26: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
27: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
28:   characters.
29: 
30: #### Circumflex
31: 
32: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
33: 
34: #### Breve
35: 
36: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
37: 
38: #### Horn
39: 
40: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
41: 
42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
173:   Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
175:   new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
176:   characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
177: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
178: 
179: #### Override rules
180: 
181: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
182:   character literally.
183: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
184:   their lower cases.
185: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
186: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
187: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
188:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
189:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
190: 
191: #### Scan pseudo-word
192: 
193: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
194:   the tone in the correct position.
195: - The existing characters must not be empty.
196: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
197: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
198:   character to scan, then stop.
199: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
200:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
201: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
202:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
203:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
204: 
205: #### Tone position rules
206: 
207: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
208: 
209: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
210:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
211: - Exceptions:
212:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
213: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
214: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
215:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
216:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
217:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
218:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
219:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
220:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
221:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
222:     consonants, put on second vowel.
223:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
224: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
225: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking: I need to look through the rest of the test file to see how tone handling is currently being tested.
I'm looking for existing test cases that follow the same pattern, especially ones testing cancel behavior in the rising tone context.

I'm reviewing the production code for the `'S', 's'` case to understand how the rising tone cancellation works, so I can write a test that covers the representative cases where `State.add` should cancel an existing rising tone and switch to literal input. Now I'm laying out the test assertions to verify the cancellation behavior: the buffer grows by one character, the modification index points to the vowel that was reset, literal_index marks where the trigger was appended, and the previously-rising tone becomes level while everything else stays level. I need to check how `reset_tone` sets the modification index, then design test cases starting with simple scenarios like a single vowel with rising tone triggered by 's'. Defining test cases... Setting up representative test cases that mirror the apply-rising pattern, using Span directly to seed the buffer without a helper struct. Each case exercises the cancellation behavior: resetting the tone to level, marking the literal index, appending the trigger character, and verifying the buffer state and mode remain consistent across lowercase/uppercase triggers, single and multi-vowel scenarios.

content.text:
Here's my proposed approach for the test before I consult the Oracle.

## What the test exercises

Per spec (Tones → Cancel rules) and the production branch at `'S', 's'` in `State.add` (lex.zig lines 760–770), the cancellation path for rising tone runs when:

- `mode == .telex`, `buffer_length > 0`, `literal_index == null`,
- the pseudo-word contains at least one vowel,
- the word is not exact `QU`,
- the existing tone on the pseudo-word's vowel is exactly `.rising`,
- and the trigger is `s` / `S`.

The expected effects:
1. `reset_tone` flips that vowel back to `.level` (case + diacritic preserved) and sets `buffer_modification_index` to that vowel's index.
2. `literal_index` is set to the current `buffer_length` (before appending).
3. The trigger char is appended literally (`Span.init(c)`).
4. `mode` stays `.telex`.

## Test shape (mirrors "apply rising tone for representative cases")

Per your note: no `SeedSpan`. Use `Span` directly.

```diagram
╭───────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Case = struct {                               │
│   seeds: []const Span,                        │
│   trigger: u8,                                │
│   rising_index: u8,  // where rising sits     │
│ }                                             │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

Setup per case: `state.init()`, `state.mode = .telex`, copy `seeds` into `buffer_effective`, `buffer_length = seeds.len`. Act: `state.add(trigger)`.

## Representative cases (parallel to the apply-rising test)

1. Lowercase trigger, single vowel: `á` + `s` → rising_index 0.
2. Uppercase trigger, single vowel: `Á` + `S` → rising_index 0.
3. Consonant + single vowel: `bá` + `s` → rising_index 1.
4. Exact `OA` multi-vowel (rising on first vowel by tone-position rules): `hóa` + `s` → rising_index 1.
5. Trailing-suffix multi-syllable (rising lands on last syllable's vowel via pseudo-word scope): `vanhóa` + `s` → rising_index 4.

## Assertions per case

- `buffer_length == seeds.len + 1`
- `buffer_modification_index == rising_index`
- `literal_index == seeds.len`
- `mode == .telex`
- For each seed i: base preserved, diacritic preserved, tone == `.level` (the originally-rising vowel is the only one that changes; others are already level).
- New Span at `seeds.len`: `base == trigger`, `diacritic == .empty`, `tone == .level`.

Now consulting the Oracle.

input.task:
Critique the proposed plan for a Zig unit test. DO NOT write or output any example code, code blocks, snippets, pseudo-code, or skeleton. Respond with prose only. If you mention identifiers, fields, or values, do so inline in sentences. Focus on: completeness of representative cases, correctness of expected post-conditions, hidden risks, and missing assertions.

Context:
- This test file is /<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig (a Vietnamese Telex input method engine in Zig).
- The spec is /<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md.
- The empty test stub I must populate is named: "expect State.add cancels an existing rising tone for representative cases and switch to literal input".
- I cannot modify production code, only write the test body. I must seed the buffer using existing Span constructors (no new helper struct).
- The test must mirror the conventions and structure of existing tests, particularly "expect State.add apply rising tone for representative cases" and "expect State.add overrides an existing non-rising tone with rising".

The branch under test is the cancellation arm in the 'S','s' handler (lex.zig lines ~760-770). It runs when state.mode is .telex, state.buffer_length > 0, state.literal_index is null, the pseudo-word has vowels, the word is not exact 'QU', and the existing tone on the pseudoword's vowel matches the triggered tone (.rising). Effect: reset_tone flips the vowel back to .level (preserving case + diacritic) and sets buffer_modification_index to that vowel's index; literal_index is set to the pre-append buffer_length; the trigger char is appended literally; mode stays .telex.

My proposed plan:
1. Use a parametric Case struct with fields: seeds: []const Span, trigger: u8, rising_index: u8.
2. Seed buffer_effective by copying seeds Span values directly. Set state.mode = .telex, state.buffer_length = seeds.len.
3. Representative cases (5):
   (a) lowercase 'á' + 's', rising_index 0
   (b) uppercase 'Á' + 'S', rising_index 0
   (c) consonant + vowel: 'bá' + 's', rising_index 1
   (d) multi-vowel exact OA: 'hóa' + 's' with rising on 'o' (first vowel per OA rule), rising_index 1
   (e) trailing-suffix: 'vanhóa' + 's' with rising on the last 'o' per pseudoword scope, rising_index 4
4. Assertions per case: buffer_length == seeds.len + 1; buffer_modification_index == rising_index; literal_index == seeds.len; mode == .telex; for each seed index i, base and diacritic preserved and tone == .level; new Span at index seeds.len has base == trigger, diacritic .empty, tone .level.

Questions:
- Are the five representative cases sufficient and well-chosen to mirror the apply-rising test, or are there cancellation-specific cases I should add (e.g. preservation of an existing diacritic on the cancelled vowel, or a case where the rising tone sits on a vowel that has a circumflex/horn)?
- Are my assertions complete? Anything I'm missing about state invariants after cancellation (e.g. asserting the new appended Span explicitly, checking we did NOT touch unrelated spans, anything around the case where multiple spans before the rising vowel must remain unchanged)?
- Any subtle correctness pitfalls in my expected values for buffer_modification_index or literal_index?
- Is using rising_index as an explicit Case field cleaner than deriving from the seeds, or does it create double-source-of-truth that could mask bugs?

Please reply in prose only, no example code at all.

run.result:
1. TL;DR: Your plan is structurally good and matches the nearby tests, but I would not consider it complete as written. The main gap is that it does not explicitly prove cancellation preserves an existing vowel diacritic, and your trailing-suffix case is weaker than it looks for proving pseudo-word scoping.

2. Recommended approach, simple path:  
First, keep the overall shape: a small table of representative cases, direct seeding with existing Span constructors, one call to State.add, then full-state assertions. That mirrors the existing apply-rising test well.  
Second, keep most of your five cases, but replace one of the plainer ones with a case where the cancelled rising tone sits on a vowel that already has a non-empty diacritic, ideally a horn or circumflex. That is the most cancellation-specific behavior your current set misses.  
Third, treat the multi-vowel cases as “representative multi-vowel shapes,” not as proof of tone-placement rules. This test is about cancellation plus switching to literal input, not about where rising should be placed originally.  
Fourth, strengthen the suffix-scope case if you want it to justify its inclusion. As currently described, it only shows a later vowel is reset; it does not truly prove earlier spans outside the scanned pseudo-word were excluded.  
Effort is small, under an hour.

3. Rationale and trade-offs:  
Your current five cases do cover several useful axes: lowercase and uppercase triggers, index zero and nonzero modification points, a multi-vowel word, and a longer buffer where the affected vowel is not near the front. That is a good start.  
The gap is that cancellation is implemented through reset_tone, and reset_tone’s distinctive promise is that it preserves base, case, and diacritic while dropping only the tone. None of your listed cases really stress the diacritic-preservation part. Both “á” and “Á” only test case preservation on plain vowels. “hóa” and “vanhóa” still use a plain “o”, so they do not cover the riskiest preservation behavior.  
Also, your OA commentary overstates what this test can prove. In this branch, the engine does not recalculate tone placement. It scans the pseudo-word, finds the existing non-level tone, and resets it. So an OA-shaped case is still useful as a multi-vowel example, but it is not evidence that the OA placement rule is being respected here. That rule belongs to apply_tone tests, not this one.  
Similarly, the suffix case does not really prove scoping unless there is something earlier that would visibly expose an over-wide reset. If every earlier span is already level and otherwise ordinary, a buggy implementation that reset too much could still appear to pass.

4. Risks and guardrails:  
The most important hidden risk is false confidence from the trailing-suffix case. If you want that case to prove pseudo-word scope, you need an earlier out-of-scope span that would visibly change if the reset were too broad. Otherwise the case mostly duplicates “nonzero modification index in a longer word.”  
A second risk is the double-source-of-truth problem with a separate rising_index field. Because your seeds already encode which span starts with the rising tone, a separate index can drift from the seeded data. That is more of a test-maintenance risk than a production-bug masking risk, but it is real. The cleanest approach is to derive the expected modified index from the one seeded span that carries rising, or at least validate that the case metadata matches the seeded tone location. If you keep the field, think of it as expected_modification_index, not as an independent description of the input.  
A third risk is using comments that claim tone-position-rule coverage. That can mislead future readers about what this unit test actually protects. Keep the comments modest: this test proves State.add takes the cancellation path, resets the existing rising tone to level, appends the trigger literally, sets literal_index, and leaves mode as telex.  
On assertions, your current list is mostly right. The key guardrails are: final buffer_length must increase by one, buffer_modification_index must point to the cancelled vowel’s absolute buffer index, literal_index must equal the pre-append buffer length, mode must remain telex, the appended trigger span must be plain literal, and every preexisting span other than tone on the cancelled vowel must remain unchanged. The last part is worth stating explicitly as “untouched spans remain exactly as seeded,” not just as a collection of individual field checks.

5. When to consider the advanced path:  
Only go beyond this if you want the cancellation test to become a stronger scoping test rather than just a representative dispatch test. The trigger for that is if you suspect bugs around pseudo-word boundaries or resets leaking into earlier syllables. In that case, make one representative case include an earlier out-of-scope toned or otherwise distinctive vowel and assert it remains untouched.

6. Optional advanced path:  
A slightly stronger version of the same test would keep the same table-driven structure but make one long-buffer case prove two things at once: that the cancelled vowel can sit on a diacritic-bearing span, and that an earlier out-of-scope vowel remains unchanged. That gives you real coverage of both reset preservation and pseudo-word scoping without expanding into many more cases.

Direct answers to your questions:  
Your five cases are close, but I would add or substitute one cancellation-specific diacritic-bearing case. If you do only one extra thing, do that.  
Your assertions are mostly complete, but the missing high-value assertion is really “the cancelled span keeps its original diacritic,” plus a stronger “out-of-scope earlier span was not touched” if you keep the suffix case for scoping.  
Your expected values for buffer_modification_index and literal_index are conceptually correct. The subtlety is that both are absolute buffer indices, and literal_index is the old buffer_length before append, which equals the appended span’s index after the call.  
Using rising_index as a separate field is readable but mildly risky. Deriving it from the seeded rising Span is cleaner. If you keep it, add an internal consistency check in the test setup so the metadata cannot silently drift from the seed data.

input.pattern: expect State.add apply rising tone for representative cases|expect State.add overrides an existing non-rising tone with rising|expect State.add cancels an existing rising tone for representative cases

result: /<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2421:test "expect State.add apply rising tone for representative cases" {
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2474:test "expect State.add overrides an existing non-rising tone with rising" {
/<redacted>/lex/src/lex.zig:2511:test "expect State.add cancels an existing rising tone for representative cases and switch to literal input" {

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the following characters start the word (when the word is empty), change to literal
10:   input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W`, `Z` (ignore cases), because these characters
11:   don't start a Vietnamese word. This rule is higher priority than diacritics and tones rules.
12: - When any of the following characters are added to the word, change to literal input from the new
13:   character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal
14:   Vietnamese spelling. `Z` exists in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_... This rule is
15:   lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
16: - Literal input remains until the new word.
17: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
18: 
19: ### Diacritics
20: 
21: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
22: Tones have a separate section.
23: 
24: #### Empty
25: 
26: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
27: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
28:   characters.
29: 
30: #### Circumflex
31: 
32: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
33: 
34: #### Breve
35: 
36: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
37: 
38: #### Horn
39: 
40: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
41: 
42: #### Stroke
43: 
44: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
45: 
46: #### Apply rules
47: 
48: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
49: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
50:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
51:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
52:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
53: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
54:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
55:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
56:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
57: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
58: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
59: 
60: #### Override rules
61: 
62: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
63:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
64:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
65: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
66: - Keep tone untouched.
67: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
68: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
69: 
70: #### Cancel rules
71: 
72: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
73:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
74: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
75:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
76:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
77:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
78:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
79: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
80: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
81:   remains until the new word.
82:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
83: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
84: 
85: #### Auto-fill rules
86: 
87: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
88:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
89: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
90:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
91:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
92:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
93: - The new input character is appended literally.
94: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
95: 
96: ### Tones
97: 
98: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
99: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
100: These vowel could take any tone.
101: 
102: #### Level (ngang)
103: 
104: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
105:   corresponding variants.
106: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
107: 
108: #### Rising (sắc)
109: 
110: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
112:   -> `Ớ`.
113: 
114: #### Falling (huyền)
115: 
116: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
118:   -> `Ờ`.
119: 
120: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
121: 
122: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
124:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
125: 
126: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
127: 
128: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
129: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
130:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
131: 
132: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
133: 
134: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
135: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
136:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
137: 
138: #### Apply rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
143:   their lower cases.
144: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
145: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
146: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
147:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
148: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
149: 
150: #### Cancel rules
151: 
152: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
153:   character literally.
154: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
155:   their lower cases.
156: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
157: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
158: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
159:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
160: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
161:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
162: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
163: 
164: #### Reset rules
165: 
166: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
167:   character literally.
168: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
169: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
170: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
171:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
172: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append the new character literally and remain
173:   Vietnamese input to continue process Vietnamese input such as `dzu + x` -> `dzũ`.
174: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), append the
175:   new character literally and switch to literal from the new character position and the following
176:   characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
177: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
178: 
179: #### Override rules
180: 
181: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
182:   character literally.
183: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
184:   their lower cases.
185: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
186: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
187: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
188:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
189:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
190: 
191: #### Scan pseudo-word
192: 
193: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
194:   the tone in the correct position.
195: - The existing characters must not be empty.
196: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
197: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
198:   character to scan, then stop.
199: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
200:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
201: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
202:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
203:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
204: 
205: #### Tone position rules
206: 
207: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
208: 
209: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
210:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
211: - Exceptions:
212:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
213: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
214: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
215:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on the rightmost `Ơ`.
216:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the rightmost
217:     listed vowel found by the reverse scan.
218:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
219:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
220:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
221:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
222:     consonants, put on second vowel.
223:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
224: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
225: - Could not put tone for other cases.

progress.message:
1. TL;DR: Your plan is structurally good and matches the nearby tests, but I would not consider it complete as written. The main gap is that it does not explicitly prove cancellation preserves an existing vowel diacritic, and your trailing-suffix case is weaker than it looks for proving pseudo-word scoping.

2. Recommended approach, simple path:  
First, keep the overall shape: a small table of representative cases, direct seeding with existing Span constructors, one call to State.add, then full-state assertions. That mirrors the existing apply-rising test well.  
Second, keep most of your five cases, but replace one of the plainer ones with a case where the cancelled rising tone sits on a vowel that already has a non-empty diacritic, ideally a horn or circumflex. That is the most cancellation-specific behavior your current set misses.  
Third, treat the multi-vowel cases as “representative multi-vowel shapes,” not as proof of tone-placement rules. This test is about cancellation plus switching to literal input, not about where rising should be placed originally.  
Fourth, strengthen the suffix-scope case if you want it to justify its inclusion. As currently described, it only shows a later vowel is reset; it does not truly prove earlier spans outside the scanned pseudo-word were excluded.  
Effort is small, under an hour.

3. Rationale and trade-offs:  
Your current five cases do cover several useful axes: lowercase and uppercase triggers, index zero and nonzero modification points, a multi-vowel word, and a longer buffer where the affected vowel is not near the front. That is a good start.  
The gap is that cancellation is implemented through reset_tone, and reset_tone’s distinctive promise is that it preserves base, case, and diacritic while dropping only the tone. None of your listed cases really stress the diacritic-preservation part. Both “á” and “Á” only test case preservation on plain vowels. “hóa” and “vanhóa” still use a plain “o”, so they do not cover the riskiest preservation behavior.  
Also, your OA commentary overstates what this test can prove. In this branch, the engine does not recalculate tone placement. It scans the pseudo-word, finds the existing non-level tone, and resets it. So an OA-shaped case is still useful as a multi-vowel example, but it is not evidence that the OA placement rule is being respected here. That rule belongs to apply_tone tests, not this one.  
Similarly, the suffix case does not really prove scoping unless there is something earlier that would visibly expose an over-wide reset. If every earlier span is already level and otherwise ordinary, a buggy implementation that reset too much could still appear to pass.

4. Risks and guardrails:  
The most important hidden risk is false confidence from the trailing-suffix case. If you want that case to prove pseudo-word scope, you need an earlier out-of-scope span that would visibly change if the reset were too broad. Otherwise the case mostly duplicates “nonzero modification index in a longer word.”  
A second risk is the double-source-of-truth problem with a separate rising_index field. Because your seeds already encode which span starts with the rising tone, a separate index can drift from the seeded data. That is more of a test-maintenance risk than a production-bug masking risk, but it is real. The cleanest approach is to derive the expected modified index from the one seeded span that carries rising, or at least validate that the case metadata matches the seeded tone location. If you keep the field, think of it as expected_modification_index, not as an independent description of the input.  
A third risk is using comments that claim tone-position-rule coverage. That can mislead future readers about what this unit test actually protects. Keep the comments modest: this test proves State.add takes the cancellation path, resets the existing rising tone to level, appends the trigger literally, sets literal_index, and leaves mode as telex.  
On assertions, your current list is mostly right. The key guardrails are: final buffer_length must increase by one, buffer_modification_index must point to the cancelled vowel’s absolute buffer index, literal_index must equal the pre-append buffer length, mode must remain telex, the appended trigger span must be plain literal, and every preexisting span other than tone on the cancelled vowel must remain unchanged. The last part is worth stating explicitly as “untouched spans remain exactly as seeded,” not just as a collection of individual field checks.

5. When to consider the advanced path:  
Only go beyond this if you want the cancellation test to become a stronger scoping test rather than just a representative dispatch test. The trigger for that is if you suspect bugs around pseudo-word boundaries or resets leaking into earlier syllables. In that case, make one representative case include an earlier out-of-scope toned or otherwise distinctive vowel and assert it remains untouched.

6. Optional advanced path:  
A slightly stronger version of the same test would keep the same table-driven structure but make one long-buffer case prove two things at once: that the cancelled vowel can sit on a diacritic-bearing span, and that an earlier out-of-scope vowel remains unchanged. That gives you real coverage of both reset preservation and pseudo-word scoping without expanding into many more cases.

Direct answers to your questions:  
Your five cases are close, but I would add or substitute one cancellation-specific diacritic-bearing case. If you do only one extra thing, do that.  
Your assertions are mostly complete, but the missing high-value assertion is really “the cancelled span keeps its original diacritic,” plus a stronger “out-of-scope earlier span was not touched” if you keep the suffix case for scoping.  
Your expected values for buffer_modification_index and literal_index are conceptually correct. The subtlety is that both are absolute buffer indices, and literal_index is the old buffer_length before append, which equals the appended span’s index after the call.  
Using rising_index as a separate field is readable but mildly risky. Deriving it from the seeded rising Span is cleaner. If you keep it, add an internal consistency check in the test setup so the metadata cannot silently drift from the seed data.

content.text:
## Synthesis (mine + Oracle)

The Oracle agrees on the overall shape (table-driven, direct `Span` seeding, mirrors the apply-rising test) but flags four refinements I'll fold in:

### Refinements to apply

1. **Add a diacritic-bearing case.** Cancellation runs through `reset_tone`, whose distinctive guarantee is preserving base, case, and diacritic while only stripping the tone. None of my five cases stress diacritic preservation. Substitute or add one case where the rising vowel carries a non-empty diacritic — e.g. seed `tiế` (`t`, `i`, `ê` with rising), trigger `s`, expected: `t`, `i`, `ê` (circumflex preserved, tone level), then literal `s`. rising_index = 2.

2. **Demote the OA framing.** This branch does not re-run tone-position rules; it only resets whatever non-level tone is found. So `hóa` is fine as a representative multi-vowel shape, but the comment must not claim it proves OA placement (that belongs to `apply_tone` tests).

3. **Strengthen or trim the trailing-suffix case.** As described, `vanhóa` only proves "modification at a non-zero index in a longer buffer." To make it actually prove pseudo-word scoping, I'd need an earlier out-of-scope distinctive span (e.g. an earlier vowel with its own diacritic) and assert it remains exactly as seeded. Otherwise it's redundant — drop it or upgrade it.

4. **Avoid double source of truth on `rising_index`.** Either derive the expected modification index from the single seed Span carrying `.rising`, or rename the field to `expected_modification_index` and have the setup loop assert the seed at that index actually has `.rising` so the metadata can't drift from the seeded data.

### Assertion guardrails (also from Oracle)

- All untouched spans remain exactly as seeded (state explicitly, not just per-field).
- Appended trigger Span at the post-append last index is plain literal (`base == trigger`, `.empty`, `.level`).
- `buffer_modification_index` is the absolute buffer index of the cancelled vowel.
- `literal_index` equals the pre-append `buffer_length` (i.e., the index of the appended trigger).
- `mode` stays `.telex`; `buffer_length` grows by exactly one.

### Final case list I propose

1. Lowercase trigger, single plain vowel: seeded `á` + `s`, expected_modification_index 0.
2. Uppercase trigger, single plain vowel: seeded `Á` + `S`, expected_modification_index 0.
3. Consonant + plain vowel: seeded `bá` + `s`, expected_modification_index 1.
4. **Diacritic-bearing rising vowel** (new, replaces or augments OA): seeded `tiế` (`t`, `i`, `ê` with circumflex + rising) + `s`, expected_modification_index 2 — proves circumflex is preserved while only the tone is reset.
5. Multi-vowel representative: seeded `hóa` + `s`, expected_modification_index 1 — keep as a multi-vowel shape (comment scoped to "representative multi-vowel," no OA-rule claim).
6. Optional scoping case: keep `vanhóa` only if I upgrade an earlier span to be distinctively diacritic-bearing or otherwise observable, and add an explicit "earlier span unchanged" assertion. Otherwise drop it as redundant with case 3.

Awaiting your confirmation on:
- Including case 6 (upgraded scoping) vs. dropping it.
- Whether to keep `rising_index` as `expected_modification_index` with a setup-time consistency assert, or derive it inline from the seeds.

My comments:

- Add cases about trailing consonant(s) to representation cases.

Let me preview your plan.

## Updated plan preview

### Refinements (carrying over Oracle feedback + your new note)

- Add a diacritic-bearing rising vowel to prove `reset_tone` preserves diacritic.
- Drop OA-rule claims in comments (this branch does not re-place tones).
- Add **trailing-consonant** cases (your new requirement) so the pseudo-word is `<consonant><vowel><consonant(s)>`, not just open syllables. This proves the cancellation path works when the rising vowel is mid-buffer with consonants after it.
- Resolve the `rising_index` double-source-of-truth: rename to `expected_modification_index` and have the seeding loop assert the seed at that index actually carries `.rising`.

### Test shape (unchanged)

```diagram
╭───────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Case = struct {                                   │
│   seeds: []const Span,                            │
│   trigger: u8,                                    │
│   expected_modification_index: u8,                │
│ }                                                 │
╰───────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

Per-case flow: `state.init()` → `state.mode = .telex` → copy seeds → `buffer_length = seeds.len` → assert seed at `expected_modification_index` has `.rising` (setup guard) → `state.add(trigger)`.

### Final case list

1. Lowercase trigger, single plain vowel: `á` + `s`, expected_modification_index 0.
2. Uppercase trigger, single plain vowel: `Á` + `S`, expected_modification_index 0.
3. Consonant + plain vowel (open syllable): `bá` + `s`, expected_modification_index 1.
4. **Trailing consonant** (closed syllable): `bán` + `s`, expected_modification_index 1. Proves the rising vowel can sit before trailing consonants and only the vowel's tone is reset (the trailing `n` is untouched).
5. **Trailing consonant cluster**: `bánh` + `s`, expected_modification_index 1. Proves multi-character trailing consonants (`nh`) stay untouched.
6. **Diacritic-bearing rising vowel + trailing consonant**: `tiến` + `s` (`t`, `i`, `ê` circumflex + rising, `n`), expected_modification_index 2. Proves circumflex preserved on the cancelled vowel and the trailing `n` untouched.
7. Multi-vowel representative (open): `hóa` + `s`, expected_modification_index 1. Comment scoped to "representative multi-vowel"; no OA-rule claim.

(Dropping the original `vanhóa` scoping case as redundant with cases 4–6, which already cover trailing consonants and longer buffers without needing a contrived earlier-span guard.)

### Per-case assertions

- `buffer_length == seeds.len + 1`
- `buffer_modification_index == c.expected_modification_index`
- `literal_index == seeds.len`
- `mode == .telex`
- For each seed index `i`:
  - `base` preserved, `diacritic` preserved.
  - `tone == .level` for the cancelled vowel; all other seeds were already `.level` and stay `.level` (i.e., every seed ends `.level`).
- Appended Span at index `seeds.len`: `base == trigger`, `diacritic == .empty`, `tone == .level`.

Awaiting your confirmation before writing the test body.

content.thinking:
Now I'm setting up test cases to verify the modification index logic, covering accented vowels, uppercase variants, and different consonant patterns at the end of words. Continuing with test cases...

I need to verify that `literal_index` starts as null before seeding, which the default initialization handles. Looking at the `init()` function, it sets `buffer_length` to 0 and leaves `literal_index` null by default, though I'll need to explicitly set the mode to `.telex` for these tests. The assertions in `State.add` should pass since our seeded `literal_index` is null and the buffer constraints are satisfied.

When I call `add('s')`, it enters the 's','s' branch. With `literal_index` null and `mode` set to `.telex`, the function scans backward through the pseudoword, finds the rising tone on a vowel, and calls `reset_tone()` to clear that tone and set `buffer_modification_index` to the vowel's position. Then it sets `literal_index` to the current buffer length before appending the trigger character as a new span. I should verify that `reset_tone` actually sets `buffer_modification_index` correctly.
Let me double-check my understanding of reset_tone.

I've confirmed that `reset_tone` sets `buffer_modification_index` to the first vowel with non-level tone, so now I'm writing the test and checking the assertion style used in similar tests to match the casting pattern.

Writing test assertions...

content.text:
The new test (28/33) passes. The two failing tests (20 and 23) are pre-existing failures unrelated to my changes — they are about `Z` and `F/J/W/Z` handling, which the production code currently leaves stubbed (`'Z', 'z' => {}` at line 918) and which my edit did not touch.

Summary:
- Added test "expect State.add cancels an existing rising tone for representative cases and switch to literal input" with 8 cases: lowercase/uppercase plain vowel, consonant + vowel, trailing consonant `bán`, trailing cluster `bánh`, diacritic-bearing `tiến`, multi-vowel `hóa`, and longer-buffer `vanhóa`.
- Includes a setup-time guard asserting the seed at `expected_modification_index` carries `.rising`, eliminating the double-source-of-truth risk the Oracle flagged.
- Verified: the new test passes; the two unrelated pre-existing failures concern unimplemented `Z`/`F`/`J`/`W` branches.
